titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Plath Family Papers
titlestmt.author: Matthew Gorham and Elise Riley

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This collection contains 1 published digital object. And that object are comprised of 32 digital files.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday January 28, 2026 at 00:27

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Plath family papers document the life and work of American poet Sylvia Plath, her relationship with her family, the lives of her parents, Aurelia and Otto Plath, and her brother, Warren Plath. The papers consist of correspondence, personal and professional papers of members of the Plath family, photographs, and audiovisual materials.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Materials in Box 49 are restricted until January 1, 2059.
Materials in Boxes 54 and 55 are closed to research pending conservation treatment/housing.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from James S. Jaffe Rare Books LLC on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2025.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in four series: I. Correspondence, 1911-2017; II. Plath family papers, 1884-2019; III. Photographs, 1890-1994; IV. Audiovisual materials, 1958-1999.

bioghist.head: Sylvia Plath (1932-1963)
bioghist.p: Sylvia Plath was an American poet, best known for the poetry collections, The Colossus and Other Poems (1960) and Ariel (1965), and the novel, The Bell Jar (1963). Plath was born in Boston, Massachusetts on October 27, 1932 to Aurelia Schober Plath and Otto Plath. Her brother, Warren Plath was born in 1935. While Plath was growing up, her family lived in Jamaica Plain, Winthrop, and Wellesley, Massachusetts.

Plath attended Smith College, and after graduation, attended Newnham College at Cambridge University on a Fulbright fellowship. While at Cambridge, she met English poet Ted Hughes, and the two were married on June 16, 1956. Following Plath's graduation from Cambridge in 1957, she and Hughes relocated to the United States until 1959, then they returned to England.

After returning to England, Plath published Colossus and Other Poems , wrote The Bell Jar , and completed the poems that would comprise Ariel . Additionally, Plath's and Hughes' children, Frieda Hughes (b. April 1, 1960) and Nicholas Hughes (b. January 17, 1962) were born during this period.

Plath lived with and was treated for clinical depression throughout her adult life, and depictions of mental illness were a recurrent theme in her writing. Sylvia Plath died by suicide in London on February 11, 1963.
In 1975, a collection of Plath's letters to her family, compiled by Aurelia Plath, was published as Letters Home . In 1981, her Collected Poems was posthumously awarded the Pulitzer Prize.

bioghist.head: Ted Hughes (1930-1998)
bioghist.p: Ted Hughes was an English poet, husband of Sylvia Plath, and father of their two children, Frieda and Nicholas Hughes. Born on August 17, 1930, Hughes grew up in West Yorkshire, England. He had two siblings, Gerald Hughes and Olwyn Hughes. Hughes attended Pembroke College at Cambridge University, where he studied English, anthropology, and archaeology. His poetry was widely published throughout his lifetime, and in 1984, he was appointed Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom, a title he held until his death.

In 1962, while married to Sylvia Plath, Hughes began an extramarital affair with writer Assia Wevill, which resulted in his and Plath's separation. Hughes and Wevill remained partnered after Plath's death and had one daughter together, Alexandra (Shura) Hughes, born in 1965. Both Assia Wevill and Shura Hughes died on March 23, 1969 in a murder-suicide carried out by Wevill. In 1970, Hughes married Carol Orchard, and they remained married until Hughes' death on October 28, 1998.

bioghist.head: Aurelia Plath (1906-1994)
bioghist.p: Aurelia Plath was an American university professor, wife of Otto Plath, and mother of Sylvia and Warren Plath. She was born Aurelia Schober on April 26, 1906, in Boston to parents Frank Schober and Aurelia Greenwood Schober. She had two siblings, Dorothy Schober (later Dorothy Benotti) and Frank Schober, Jr.

Plath attended Boston University's College of Practical Arts and Letters and graduated with a Bachelor of Secretarial Sciences degree in 1928. She subsequently completed a Master of Arts degree in English and German at Boston University in 1930. She married Otto Plath in 1932, and their children, Sylvia and Warren Plath were born in 1932 and 1935, respectively.

Following Otto Plath's death in 1940, Aurelia began working as an instructor of medical secretarial skills at Boston University and attained the rank of associate professor. Plath retired from teaching in 1971. In the later years of her life, she was actively involved in the stewardship of Sylvia Plath's posthumous legacy, and compiled the collection, Letters Home , published in 1975. Aurelia Plath died on March 11, 1994.

bioghist.head: Otto Plath (1885-1940)
bioghist.p: Otto Plath was a German-American entomologist and university professor, husband of Aurelia Plath, and father of Sylvia and Warren Plath.

Otto Plath was born on April 13, 1885, in Grabow, Germany, the oldest of six children. In 1900 at the age of 15, Plath emigrated to the United States, initially living with an uncle in New York City before moving to Wisconsin to live with his grandparents. From 1906 to 1910, Plath attended college at Northwestern University, earning a bachelor's degree in classical languages. Following a brief tenure at the Wisconsin Lutheran Seminary, Plath moved to Seattle and earned a Master of Arts degree from the University of Washington. Beginning in 1922, Plath taught at Boston University. During this time, he completed both a Master of Science and Doctoral degree at Harvard University. In 1929, he met Aurelia Schober while she was a graduate student at Boston University.
Otto Plath began to suffer from a long-term illness in 1935 that was ultimately diagnosed as advanced diabetes. He died on November 5, 1940.

bioghist.head: Warren Plath (1935-2021)
bioghist.p: Warren Plath was an American computer scientist, son of Aurelia and Otto Plath, and brother of Sylvia Plath. He was born in Jamaica Plain, Massachusetts on April 27, 1935. Plath attended Philips Exeter Academy and Harvard University for both his undergraduate and graduate degrees, ultimately earning a Ph.D in 1964. He also attended the University of Bonn in Germany on a Fulbright Fellowship. An expert in the field of theoretical and computational linguistics, Plath spent his career as a researcher at IBM's T.J. Watson Research Center until his retirement in 2001.
In 1960, Plath met Margaret Wetzel and the two married in 1962. The couple had two daughters, Jennifer and Susan Plath (later Susan Winston). Warren Plath died on February 14, 2021.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Yale Archival Processing Manual.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related material: Sylvia Plath collection at Smith College (MRBC-MS-00045); Sylvia Plath collection at Emory University (Manuscript Collection No.1318); Ted Hughes papers at Emory University (Manuscript Collection No. 644); and Plath mss I-XI at the Lilly Library of Indiana University Bloomington.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Plath family papers document the life and work of American poet Sylvia Plath, her relationship with her family, the lives of her parents, Aurelia and Otto Plath, and her brother, Warren Plath. The papers consist of correspondence, personal and professional papers of members of the Plath family, photographs, and audiovisual materials.

Correspondence in the papers is notable for including letters between Sylvia Plath and her immediate family; letters between Ted Hughes and members of the Plath family; extensive correspondence between Aurelia and Warren Plath; and letters written by Ted Hughes's partner Assia Wevill to Aurelia Plath. Sylvia Plath's correspondence includes some letters that were published in Letters Home . The papers also include documentation of Sylvia Plath's death and it's immediate aftermath, including correspondence between family members, letters to the Plath family from Sylvia Plath's friends, notebooks maintained by Warren and Margaret Plath, and official documentation.

Personal and professional papers of Aurelia, Otto, and Warren Plath include diaries and notebooks, educational records, writings, clippings, and other papers. Aurelia Plath's papers include extensive documentation of the production and publication of Letters Home , which she was heavily involved in. Many of the letters featured in the book can be found in the correspondence.
Photographs document Sylvia Plath throughout her life, other Plath and Schober family members, and Ted, Frieda, and Nicholas Hughes.
Audiovisual materials include commercially-produced recordings of Sylvia Plath reading her poems, interviews with Aurelia Plath, and recordings of television programs about Sylvia Plath.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Plath family papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains correspondence of Sylvia Plath, her husband, Ted Hughes, her parents, Aurelia and Otto Plath, and her brother, Warren Plath.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in five subseries: Sylvia Plath correspondence, Ted Hughes correspondence, Aurelia Plath correspondence, Otto Plath correspondence, Warren Plath correspondence.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized into two groupings: Outgoing correspondence, Incoming correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains outgoing and incoming correspondence written by and to Sylvia Plath throughout her life. Correspondents are primarily family members. The bulk of the letters are to Sylvia's mother Aurelia and brother Warren. Also included are correspondence to and from friends, and colleagues.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains outgoing correspondence written by Sylvia Plath. The correspondence is largely to Sylvia's family members including her aunt and uncle, Dorothy and Joseph Benotti, her mother Aurelia, her brother Warren and Warren's first wife Margaret. Also included in this series are letters to a friend of her mother's, Grace Berry, and an unidentified friend Macey.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This is a photocopy of a letter Sylvia Plath wrote on September 22, 1954 in response to her childhood neighbor, Macey Feingold about their romantic troubles.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains letters from Sylvia Plath to her mother Aurelia Plath. Included in the material are childhood cards with drawings and poems as well letters that were published in Letters Home .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This note accompanied flowers given to Aurelia by Sylvia upon her mother's arrival in England.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This postcard is partially water stained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This is a fragment of a letter wherein pages 1 and 2 are missing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collage letter using colored wrapping paper.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Blank card with Sylvia Plath's initials on recto.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One letter, dated 1961, from Chalcot Square is a joint letter from Sylvia Plath and Ted Hughes to Warren Plath.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains incoming correspondence sent to Sylvia Plath. Both letters in this sub-series are copies of letters Sylvia received from editors at Harper & Row and Knopf rejecting her manuscript of The Bell Jar .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists primarily of outgoing correspondence of Ted Hughes. Correspondents include Frieda and Nicholas Hughes, Aurelia, Warren, and Margaret Plath, Elizabeth Aldrich, a childhood friend and neighbor of Sylvia Plath, and the writer Olive Prouty, who was a friend and financial benefactor of Sylvia Plath during her early adult life.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this subseries are organized alphabetically.

did.unittitle: Empty envelope addressed to Ted Hughes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains both outgoing and incoming correspondence written by and to Aurelia Plath throughout her life. Correspondents include family members, friends, colleagues, publishing industry figures, and fans and researchers of Sylvia Plath's life and work.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized into two groupings: Outgoing correspondence, Incoming correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains letters written by Aurelia Plath to family members and close friends. Notable letters include one written to Sylvia Plath (but addressed to both her and Warren Plath on the envelope) to be read on Sylvia's 15th birthday, as well as an unsent letter to Ted Hughes from the mid-1970s.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unsent letter to Ted Hughes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter written to Sylvia Plath by Aurelia Plath, to be read on Sylvia's fifteenth birthday. On the envelope in which it was enclosed, the letter is addressed to both Sylvia and Warren Plath.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of letters written to Aurelia Plath by family members, friends, colleagues, fans and researchers of Sylvia Plath's work, and publishing industry figures.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in two groupings: Personal correspondence, Correspondence related to Sylvia Plath.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of letters written to Aurelia Plath by family members, friends, and colleagues.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence related to bequests to Aurelia Plath from the estates of Olive Prouty and Hazel Purmort.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of letters written to Aurelia Plath concerning various aspects of Sylvia Plath's life and literary legacy in the years following her death. Correspondents include student and academic researchers of Sylvia Plath's life and work, fans of Sylvia Plath's poetry, and publishing industry figures.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence related to the first U.S. printing of Sylvia Plath's novel, The Bell Jar in 1971.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains outgoing and incoming correspondence written by and to Warren Plath throughout his life. Correspondents include family members, friends, and colleagues.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized into two groupings: Outgoing correspondence, Incoming correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of letters written by Warren Plath to his mother, Aurelia Plath, his sister, Sylvia Plath, and his wife, Margaret Wetzel Plath. Also includes correspondence with friends from Phillips Exeter Academy --Alex Golstein and Clement Henry-- with whom Warren sustained life-long friendships.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains letters written to Warren Plath from family, friends, and colleagues. Warren maintained friendships with his suitemates at Philips Exeter Academy as well as classmates and mentors from his studies abroad in Germany. Also included in these materials is correspondence related to his sister Sylvia Plath's life and work, including the publication of Letters Home .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized into two groupings: Outgoing correspondence, Incoming correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains outgoing and incoming correspondence written by and sent to Margaret Plath. Some of the correspondence is jointly addressed to Margaret and Warren Plath.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains outgoing correspondence written by Margaret Plath. Correspondents include friends Charlotte and the Spaldings, as well as Aurelia Plath, Margaret's mother-in-law.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains incoming correspondence sent to Margaret Plath. Correspondents include Margaret's parents, Alice and Charles Wetzel, and her friends Mary Spalding, Marilyn Lindblad, and Susan Matery. Mary's correspondence is the most significant, spanning decades with numerous letters sharing details of their personal lives.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized into two groupings: Outgoing correspondence, Incoming correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains outgoing and incoming correspondence written by and sent to Otto Plath. Correspondents include professional colleagues, friends, and family.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains correspondence written by Otto Plath. Included is a photocopy of a postcard sent to a relative, Martha, a letter discussing bumblebee speciation with Phillip Phair, and several letters to William Warren, the dean of Otto's department at Boston University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains incoming correspondence sent to Otto Plath. Included in the correspondents are friends and colleagues. Of note are letters from Hans Gaebler and William Warren with whom Otto discussed his family life as well as his work as an entomologist. The general correspondence primarily concerns the publication and reception of his book Bumble Bees and Their Ways .

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are organized into five subseries: Sylvia Plath papers, Papers related to Sylvia Plath's death, Aurelia Plath papers, Warren Plath papers, Otto Plath papers.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Materials in Box 55 are closed to research pending conservation treatment/housing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains personal and professional papers of members of the Plath family, including Sylvia Plath, her parents Aurelia and Otto Plath, and her brother Warren Plath.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this subseries are organized chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains materials created by Sylvia Plath or related to her personal life. Included in the materials are a set of silhouette portraits, fragments of writing from high school and college, and Plath's stamp collection.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Materials in Box 55 are closed to research pending conservation treatment/housing.

did.unittitle: Untitled writing and photograph of Warren Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photograph of Warren Plath and a typewritten humorous piece of writing by Sylvia Plath concerning the photograph.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A stocking gift from Christmas 1945.

did.unittitle: Papers related to Sylvia Plath's death

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains papers documenting the immediate aftermath of Sylvia Plath's death on February 11, 1963. The bulk of the materials consist of correspondence to and from Warren and Margaret Plath. Correspondents include family members, friends of Sylvia, and solicitors. There is also one folder of correspondence from Aurelia Plath to Ted Hughes. Much of the correspondence concerns the welfare of Sylvia Plath and Ted Hughes' children following Plath's death, as well as the Plath family's unsuccessful efforts to gain primary custody of them.

Additional materials include copies of Sylvia Plath's death certificates; a receipt from a hotel in London at which Warren Plath stayed when he traveled there to attend to affairs related to Sylvia's death; and two pocket-sized notebooks used by Warren and Margaret Plath during this period.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized according to document type. Correspondence in the subseries is organized alphabetically.

did.unittitle: Becker, Gerry and Jillian to Margaret and Warren Plath
Benotti, Dorothy to Margaret and Warren Plath
Farrar, Hilda to Margaret and Warren Plath
Secker-Walker, David and Lorna to Margaret and Warren Plath
Spalding, John and Mary to Margaret and Warren Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes Sylvia Plath's Entry of Death certificate, issued by the Registrar of Births and Deaths in London, as well as a Report of the Death of an American Citizen, issued by the American Embassy in London.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This subseries is organized in five groupings: Diaries and notebooks, Letters Home papers, Personal and business papers, Writings by others, Clippings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains materials related to the professional and personal life of Aurelia Plath. The materials include a series of diaries and notebooks Aurelia kept for most of her adult life which document her daily life and personal thoughts. Letters Home papers contains materials related to the publication of the book, Letters Home which is an edited collection of letters Sylvia and Aurelia exchanged from 1950 to 1963. Also included in this series are records relating to Aurelia's professional life as an associate professor, writings by others, and news clippings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes diaries and notebooks Aurelia Plath wrote throughout her adult life. The diaries start when Aurelia was in college and continue until a few years before her death in 1994. Her diaries and notebooks record her day to day life as well as trips and holidays she took with family. These trips included visits to England where her daughter Sylvia Plath and her grandchildren lived.

did.unittitle: Journey to England with Dot and Joe diary

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in five groupings: Correspondence, Contracts, Publication and production materials, Income and expenses, Speaker notes and reviews.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consists of materials related to the drafting and publication of Letters Home . Included in the materials are correspondence with publishers and scholars, contracts, drafts, financial records, speaker notes, and reviews related to Letters Home .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This grouping contains correspondence related to the publication of Letters Home . Included are letters from Fran McCullough and Jim Fox, editors from Harper and Row; Olwyn Hughes, literary executor for Sylvia Plath's estate; and Rose Leiman Goldemberg, playwright who adapted Letters Home into a play.

did.unittitle: Axelrod, Steven Gould and Richard Kot
Burns, Edward T. regarding Letters Home
Butscher, Edward and book correspondence, etc.
Desmarais, Rebecca appraisal and Smith College correspondence
Dreben, Raya regarding international copyright law
Hale, Robert letter regarding talk on Letters Home given at Wellesley College
Hughes, Olwyn to Aurelia, Warren, and Margaret Plath
Kroll, Judith regarding Chapters in a Mythology
McCormick, Dennis regarding [Aurelia's] talk to his English class
Payne, Karen regarding Letters between Mothers and Daughters
General correspondence regarding Letters Home reviews
Correspondence regarding media mentions of Sylvia Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This grouping contains legal and financial material related to the publication of Letters Home . Included are contracts with publishing houses Harper and Row, Faber and Faber, as well as German and French publishers for translated editions, notes, and a small amount of correspondence. The correspondence and contracts discuss the copyright of Sylvia Plath's writing. Also included are the contracts for the play adaption of Letters Home written by Rose Leiman Goldemberg.

did.unittitle: Harper and Row financial reports on advances
Preliminary contract drafts with Harper and Row
Contract with Faber and Faber regarding Letters Home
Harper and Row Letters Home contracts
Raya Dreben on copyrights after author's death
Contracts with Ted Hughes regarding Letters Home
Harper and Row, Assistant General Counsel legal letters regarding Letters Home
Harper and Row Letters Home contract amendments
Contracts with William Young and Company
William Young and Company contracts, inventory, and sales
Faber and Faber Letters Home contracts and royalties
Carl Hanser Verlag and foreign publications
Contracts for play: Letters Home by Rose Leiman Goldemberg
Amendment to play contract with Rose Leiman Goldemberg
Tierce, French translation of Letters Home
Regarding French translation of Letters Home

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains material related to the drafting process of Letters Home as well as production materials for adaptations of Sylvia Plath's works. The bulk of the materials are copies, carbons, and typescripts of Sylvia Plath's letters and poems with annotations made by Aurelia Plath which provide contextual information for how to include the material in the book Letters Home .

did.unittitle: Copies of Mrs. Prouty's letters to insert in Letters Home
Carbons of Mrs. Prouty's excerpted letters
"Ocean 1212-W" from Sylvia Plath's BBC talk on childhood recollections
Copies of Sylvia Plath's correspondence with Hans-Joachim Neupert
Copies of Sylvia's poems with notes
Carbons of Sylvia Plath's poems of notes
Copies of "Yagwigha, on a Red Couch, Among Lilies, A Sestina for the Douanier" and "The Disquieting Muses"
Typed loose leaf poems of Sylvia's

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains material related to financial expenses and income from the publication of Letters Home . Included in the material are income statements, bills, and royalty statements.

did.unittitle: 1975 Book income and a letter from Olwyn [Hughes] regarding Faber and Faber publishing whole book
1976 Book expenses: legal fees, postage, etc.
Virginia Aldrich: 1978 book expenses, personal letters
Income from Rose Leiman Goldemberg play: Letters Home and expenses
Richard Kot and James Fox, editors from Harpers and Row and income
Letters Home play, business regarding Edward T. Burns's bills

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains material related to Aurelia Plath's speaking engagements as well as reviews and news clippings related to Letters Home . Included in the materials are notes, speaking scripts, and other preparative material for events and talks.

did.unittitle: Notes - perhaps useful in talking about Sylvia and Letters Home
Author series talk: Hathaway House Bookshop and Robert Hales' letter
Excerpts, papers, and notes for talk given at an author's series at Wellesley College
French production of Letters Home reviews

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a variety of materials documenting Aurelia Plath's personal life and professional career.

did.unittitle: Plath and Schober family geneaology materials
Stories written for Sylvia and Warren
Employment history: Boston University 1942-1970 and Cape Cod Community College 1970-1973
Phillips Exeter Academy reports and forms
Sponsorship materials for Edward [Ted] James Hughes
Note and photograph of Frieda, Nick, and Shura
Donation to Lilly Library Indiana University, books appraised by William Young and Company
Agreement of purchase Lilly Library Indiana University
Income from Lilly Library Indiana University
Correspondence from Lilly Library Indiana University, Saundra Taylor and Olwyn Hughes to William Cagle
Lilly Library Indiana University income table
Guide to Sylvia Plath MSS.11 Lilly Library Indiana University
Funeral arrangements with Geo Doherty and Sons
Cost of new furnishings for Aurelia's apartment
Voices and Visions contract with Lawrence Pitkethly and Sasha Albert
Catalogue of books not signed by Sylvia
Clothing list and notes from "Viele heissen [heißen] Kain"
Envelope from a Ted Hughes letter
Reproduction of "Rain and Flowers" by Sylvia Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains writings by others collected by Aurelia Plath. Included in the materials are manuscripts of The Journals of Sylvia Plath edited by Ted Hughes and Sylvia Plath: A Biography by Linda Wagner-Martin both containing annotations made by Aurelia, as well as an untitled poem by Ted Hughes that Aurelia pulled from the trash.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains an unpublished and untitled poem by Ted Hughes that Aurelia Plath pulled from her trash after a visit.

did.unittitle: "A Friend Recalls Sylvia Plath" from Glamour magazine by Elinor Klein
Robert Lowell: Life and Art by Stephen Gould Axelrod
The Journals of Sylvia Plath by Ted Hughes, uncorrected proof
"The Two Ariels" by Marjorie Perlofi and "Aurelia Plath and Richard Larschan" by Maeve Hickock
Articles regarding Sylvia Plath by Seamus Heany
"Aurelia Plath and Richard Larschan" by Maeve Hickock and "Sylvia Plath: Early and Limited Editions of Plath's Work Command High Prices" by Geoffrey Brown
Sylvia Plath: A Biography by Linda Wagner-Martin, British paperback edition
Sylvia Plath: A Biography by Linda Wagner-Martin, German translation

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains news clippings, reviews, and articles collected by Aurelia Plath. Included in the materials are clippings about Sylvia Plath and Ted Hughes's works as well as reviews of books written about them and clippings about Aurelia's own work and speaking engagements.

did.unittitle: Christian Science Monitor publications about Sylvia
The New Review , Elizabeth Sigmund on Sylvia Plath
Odds and ends including review of Voices and Visions
Bitter Fame clippings and letter from Linda Wagner
"Dying to Know the Worst" New York Times article
Magazine listing of articles about Sylvia Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains materials related to the personal and professional life of Warren Plath. Included are educational records from Philips Exeter Academy and Harvard University, material related to his career at IBM, and clippings of news articles related to Sylvia Plath and Ted Hughes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this subseries are organized in two groupings: Personal and professional papers, and Clippings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains materials related to the personal and professional life of Warren Plath. Included in the materials are early childhood ephemera, vital records, school records from Phillips Exeter Academy and Harvard College, and employment records and work material from IBM.

did.unittitle: Stories from the Great Metropolitan Operas by Helen Dike
Song of Robin Hood by Anne Malcolmson
Phillips Exeter Academy English and French papers
Harvard College admissions, scholarships, and commencement
"The Relative Frequency of English Consonantal Patterns" by Warren Plath

title.part: Trends in European and American Linguistics 1930-1960

did.unittitle: Class of 1953 Phillips Exeter Academy 40th Reunion book
Obituaries and tributes for Aurelia Plath
Phillips Exeter Academy 50th Reunion attendance list
Boy Scouts and NRA [National Rifle Association] ephemera

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains news clippings collected by Warren Plath about himself, his sister Sylvia Plath, and his brother-in-law Ted Hughes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this subseries are organized chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains material related to the personal and professional life of Otto Plath. Included in the materials are vital records, education records, professional papers from Otto's career as a German teacher and entomologist, and material about his death in 1940.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series primarily consists of photographs of members of the Plath family, including Sylvia Plath, her children Frieda and Nicholas Hughes, and husband Ted Hughes, as well as Aurelia Plath, Otto Plath, Warren Plath, and other family members. Also included are photographs of Plath and Schober family homes, photographs of Aurelia Plath's friends and colleagues, and photographic postcards collected by Aurelia Plath during trips to Europe.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Materials in Box 49 are restricted until January 1, 2059.
Materials in Box 54 are closed to research pending conservation treatment/housing.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are organized alphabetically.

did.unittitle: Photographs of friends and extended family
Photographs of Sylvia Plath with her children
Photographs of Sylvia Plath with Ted Hughes
Photographs of Sylvia and Warren Plath

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Materials in Box 54 are closed to research pending conservation treatment/housing.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are organized chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains audiovisual material related to Aurelia and Sylvia Plath. Included are recordings of Sylvia Plath reading her poems, interviews with Aurelia Plath, and copies of television programs about Sylvia Plath.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

did.unittitle: Plath / An Interview: Sylvia Plath talks with Peter Orr of the British Council, opened

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Commercially produced.

did.unittitle: Plath / An Interview: Sylvia Plath talks with Peter Orr of the British Council, unopened

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Commercially produced, received by by Library unopened.

did.unittitle: J.V. Women '76 / Aurelia Plath interview with / Sharon King
from Beth / "The Book Programme" / BBC - 20 April 1976
Sylvia Plath: The Dark Repose / Oct. 27, 1983 - With Love
SP-008-839 Voices and Visions on Channel 2 4/24/88 "Sylvia Plath" / Locked - Silvia Plath 1 hour / starts about 008 ends 0839 does not contain credits at the end.
Letters Home Sylvia Plath / Lu par Catherine Deneuve / Introduction lue par Madeleine Assas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Commercially produced, received by Library unopened.

did.unittitle: The Voice of the Poet: Sylvia Plath

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Commercially produced, received by by Library unopened.

did.unittitle: Interview with Mrs. Plath / Saeko Ishiguro // I requested tape / Permission was not asked!
